content:
# Luke Chapter 23 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 117-119 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_023_page_001.jpg, LUK_023_page_002.jpg, LUK_023_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And having risen-up, the whole multitude of them led him before Pilate.

**2.** And they began to accuse him, saying, "This [one] we found perverting our nation, and forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, and saying himself to be Christ, a king."

**3.** And Pilate asked him, saying, "Are you the king of the Jews?" And he, having answered him, said, "You say [it]."

**4.** And Pilate said to the chief-priests and the crowds, "I find no cause in this man."

**5.** But they were insisting, saying, "He stirs-up the people, teaching throughout the whole of Judea, having begun from Galilee even to here."

**6.** And Pilate, having heard, asked if the man is a Galilean;

**7.** and having learned-fully that he is from the jurisdiction of Herod, he sent-back him to Herod, he also being in Jerusalem in these days.

**8.** And Herod, having seen Jesus, rejoiced exceedingly, for he was wanting from a considerable [time] to see him, on-account-of hearing concerning him, and he was hoping some sign to see done by him.

**9.** And he questioned him with many words; but he answered nothing to him.

**10.** And the chief-priests and the scribes had stood, vehemently accusing him.

**11.** And having treated-with-contempt him, Herod also with his soldiers, and having mocked, having clothed [him] with splendid clothing, sent-back him to Pilate.

**12.** And both Herod and Pilate became friends with each-other on that very day; for previously they were being in enmity toward themselves.

**13.** And Pilate, having called-together the chief-priests and the rulers and the people,

**14.** said to them, "You brought to me this man as [one] turning-away the people, and behold, I, having examined [him] before you, found no cause in this man [regarding the things] of which you accuse him;

**15.** but neither [did] Herod, for he sent-back him to us; and behold, nothing worthy of death has been done by him.

**16.** Having disciplined him, therefore, I will release [him]."

**17.** [Now he had necessity to release to them one at [the] feast.]

**18.** But they cried-out all-together, saying, "Take-away this [one], and release to us Barabbas" —

**19.** who was on-account-of a certain insurrection having been-made in the city, and murder, thrown into prison.

**20.** And again Pilate called-out to them, wanting to release Jesus.

**21.** But they were shouting-back, saying, "Crucify, crucify him!"

**22.** And he said to them a third [time], "For what evil did this [one] do? No cause of death I found in him; having disciplined him, therefore, I will release [him]."

**23.** But they were pressing-upon with loud voices, asking [for] him to be crucified; and their voices were prevailing.

**24.** And Pilate decided [for] their demand to come-about;

**25.** and he released the [one] having been thrown into prison on-account-of insurrection and murder, whom they were asking-for, but Jesus he handed-over to their will.

**26.** And as they led him away, having seized Simon, a certain Cyrenian, coming from [the] field, they placed upon him the cross, to carry behind Jesus.

**27.** And there was following him a great multitude of the people, and of women who were beating [their breasts] and lamenting him.

**28.** And having turned to them, Jesus said, "Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep over me; but weep over yourselves and over your children;

**29.** because behold, days are coming in which they will say, 'Blessed [are] the barren, and the wombs which did not bear, and [the] breasts which did not nurse.'

**30.** Then they will begin to say to the mountains, 'Fall upon us,' and to the hills, 'Cover us';

**31.** because if in the green wood they do these-things, in the dry what might happen?"

**32.** And also two others, criminals, were being led with him to be put-to-death.

**33.** And when they came upon the place being-called Skull, there they crucified him, and the criminals, one on [the] right and one on [the] left.

**34.** And Jesus said, "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." And dividing his garments, they cast lots.

**35.** And the people stood watching. And the rulers also were sneering, saying, "Others he saved; let him save himself, if this is the Christ of God, the Chosen-One."

**36.** And the soldiers also mocked him, coming-near, offering-to-him vinegar,

**37.** and saying, "If you are the king of the Jews, save yourself."

**38.** And there was also an inscription over him: "The king of the Jews, this [one]."

**39.** And one of the hanging criminals was blaspheming him, saying, "Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us."

**40.** But the other, having answered, rebuked him, saying, "Do you not even fear God, because you are in the same judgment?

**41.** And we indeed justly, for [things] worthy of what we did, we are receiving-back; but this [one] did nothing out-of-place."

**42.** And he was saying, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom."

**43.** And he said to him, "Amen, I say to you, today with me you will be in Paradise."

**44.** And it was already about [the] sixth hour, and darkness came-about over the whole land until [the] ninth hour,

**45.** the sun having failed; and the veil of the temple was split [in the] middle.

**46.** And having cried-out with a loud voice, Jesus said, "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit." And having said this, he expired.

**47.** And the centurion, having seen the [thing] having happened, was glorifying God, saying, "Certainly this man was righteous."

**48.** And all the crowds having come-together-for this spectacle, having observed the [things] having happened, were returning, beating [their] breasts.

**49.** And all the [ones] known to him stood from afar, and women, the [ones] having followed-together with him from Galilee, seeing these-things.

**50.** And behold, a man named Joseph, a council-member being, a good and righteous man —

**51.** this [one] was not having consented to the counsel and deed of them — from Arimathea, a city of the Jews, who was waiting-for the kingdom of God;

**52.** this [one], having approached Pilate, asked-for the body of Jesus.

**53.** And having taken-down [it], he wrapped it in linen-cloth, and placed him in a tomb hewn-in-rock, in which no-one was yet lying.

**54.** And [the] day was [the day of] Preparation, and [the] Sabbath was dawning.

**55.** And having followed-after, the women, who were having come-together-with him out of Galilee, observed the tomb and how his body was placed.

**56.** And having returned, they prepared spices and ointments. And on the Sabbath indeed they rested according-to the commandment.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript & Folio Reference
This translation was produced from images of Codex Sinaiticus (British Library, Add. MS 43725), corresponding to Quire 74, leaves visible on PDF pages 117-119 of the digitized Sinaiticus New Testament. The text of Luke 23 begins in the fourth column of page 117 (marked "Lk. 23:7" at the bottom margin, with the chapter beginning slightly earlier in that column) and concludes on page 119.

### Critical Textual Variants

**Verse 17 — The Barabbas custom:**
Sinaiticus includes verse 17 (ΑΝΑΓΚΗΝ ΔΕ ΕΙΧΕΝ ΑΠΟΛΥΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ΚΑΤΑ ΕΟΡΤΗΝ ΕΝΑ — "Now he had necessity to release to them one at [the] feast"). However, this verse is widely considered a later harmonization drawn from Matthew 27:15 / Mark 15:6. Many critical editions (NA28, UBS5) bracket or omit it. I have retained it in brackets to signal its disputed status.

**Verse 34 — "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing":**
This is one of the most significant textual variants in the entire New Testament. In Codex Sinaiticus as it stands in its corrected state, the verse IS PRESENT. The first hand of Sinaiticus (scribe A) appears to have included this saying. Some scholars have argued that the original hand omitted it and a corrector added it, but the manuscript as visible in the images shows the text present and integrated into the flow of v.33-35 without obvious signs of insertion in the main text column. The words ΠΑΤΕΡ ΑΦΕΣ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ΟΥ ΓΑΡ ΟΙΔΑΣΙΝ ΤΙ ΠΟΙΟΥΣΙΝ are legible on page 118 (column 3). Notable witnesses OMITTING this verse include Papyrus 75 and Codex Vaticanus (B), as well as some early versions. The verse is also absent from Codex Bezae (D) in its first hand. The omission is the harder reading — one can see why a scribe might add a dominical forgiveness saying, but it is difficult to explain why one would remove it. I have retained it without brackets because Sinaiticus reads it, but scholars should be aware this is genuinely uncertain.

**Verse 34 — Division of garments / casting lots:**
Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΑΜΕΡΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΙ ΔΕ ΤΑ ΙΜΑΤΙΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΕΒΑΛΟΝ ΚΛΗΡΟΥΣ ("And dividing his garments, they cast lots") without the longer addition found in some manuscripts specifying the fulfillment of Psalm 22:18.

**Verse 38 — The inscription:**
Sinaiticus reads the inscription as Ο ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣ ΤΩΝ ΙΟΥΔΑΙΩΝ ΟΥΤΟΣ ("The king of the Jews, this [one]"), with ΟΥΤΟΣ placed at the end — a distinctive Lukan word order. Sinaiticus does NOT include the phrase ΓΡΑΜΜΑΣΙΝ ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΟΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΡΩΜΑΙΚΟΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΕΒΡΑΙΚΟΙΣ ("in Greek and Latin and Hebrew letters") found in some later manuscripts.

**Verse 42 — "Into your kingdom" vs. "In your kingdom":**
Sinaiticus reads ΕΙΣ ΤΗΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑΝ ΣΟΥ ("into your kingdom"), using the preposition εις with the accusative, suggesting directional motion — "when you come into your kingdom." Some manuscripts read ΕΝ ("in"), which would be "when you come in your kingdom." The Sinaiticus reading with εις is the more common and likely original.

**Verse 45 — "The sun having failed" (ΤΟΥ ΗΛΙΟΥ ΕΚΛΙΠΟΝΤΟΣ):**
Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΛΙΠΟΝΤΟΣ (from εκλειπω, "to fail/cease"), a genitive absolute construction. This is the reading adopted by most critical texts. Some manuscripts read ΕΣΚΟΤΙΣΘΗ ("was darkened"), which may be a harmonization to Matthew/Mark. The Sinaiticus reading with εκλειπω is notable because it is the root of our word "eclipse," though the evangelists clearly do not mean a solar eclipse (Passover occurs at full moon, making a solar eclipse astronomically impossible).

**Verse 47 — "Righteous" (ΔΙΚΑΙΟΣ) vs. "Innocent":**
Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΚΑΙΟΣ ("righteous"), which is distinctive to Luke. Matthew and Mark have the centurion say "Son of God." Luke's centurion declares Jesus ΔΙΚΑΙΟΣ — a theologically loaded term in Lukan vocabulary, connecting Jesus to the "Righteous One" tradition (cf. Acts 3:14, 7:52, 22:14).

### Translation Decisions

- **v.11 "splendid clothing" (ΕΣΘΗΤΑ ΛΑΜΠΡΑΝ):** Literally "bright/shining garment." This likely refers to a white or gleaming robe, possibly mocking royal pretension. I translated ΛΑΜΠΡΑΝ as "splendid" to preserve ambiguity between brightness and grandeur.

- **v.31 "green wood...dry":** The proverb ΕΝ ΥΓΡΩ ΞΥΛΩ...ΕΝ ΤΩ ΞΗΡΩ is rendered as literally as possible. The metaphor is debated — most likely: if the Romans do this to an innocent man (green/living wood), what will they do to the guilty (dry/dead wood)?

- **v.43 "Paradise" (ΠΑΡΑΔΕΙΣΟΣ):** Transliterated from the Greek, which itself is a loanword from Old Persian (pairidaeza, "enclosed garden"). Used in the LXX for the Garden of Eden. I retain the transliteration as the Greek text does.

- **v.45 "the sun having failed":** I translated ΤΟΥ ΗΛΙΟΥ ΕΚΛΙΠΟΝΤΟΣ as a genitive absolute, preserving the participial construction rather than converting it to a finite verb.

- **v.51 Construction:** The Greek of v.50-51 is syntactically complex, with an extended parenthetical description of Joseph interrupted by a relative clause. I have preserved this awkward construction with dashes to mark the parenthetical.

---
*Translated from the manuscript by EMET, 2026-03-05*
*Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century CE, British Library*
*The EMET Bible — Truth before beauty*
